does: This paper demonstrates four novel jailbreak attacks against vision-language models that exploit the visual modality to bypass safety alignment. The attacks include encoding harmful instructions as visual symbol sequences with decoding legends, replacing harmful objects with benign visual substitutes while requesting harmful actions using the substitute terms, replacing harmful text in images with benign words while maintaining visual context that preserves original meaning, and creating visual analogy puzzles that require inferring prohibited concepts. The authors evaluate these attacks across six frontier VLMs and find substantial success rates, with their visual cipher achieving 40.9% attack success on Claude-Haiku-4.5 compared to only 10.7% for an equivalent textual cipher, demonstrating a significant cross-modality alignment gap where safety training in the text modality fails to generalize to visual expression of harmful intent.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature of adversarial attacks and jailbreaks against large language models and multimodal systems, extending beyond text-only safety research. It responds to prior work studying alignment and safety mechanisms in language models by identifying that vision-language models inherit text-focused safety training that neglects the visual channel. The paper connects to research on adversarial examples, symbol manipulation, and visual obfuscation techniques, while also relating to broader work on multimodal understanding and cross-modal knowledge in neural networks. The cross-modality alignment gap identified here relates to prior observations that safety training may not holistically cover all input modalities, building on emerging concerns about VLM vulnerabilities.
enables: This research opens several directions for improving VLM safety and alignment. The preliminary interpretability and mitigation results suggest pathways for developing more robust safety training that accounts for visual inputs and cross-modality attack surfaces. The work enables future research into multimodal safety alignment, where defenses must consider not just individual modalities but their interactions. It points toward the need for vision-specific safety mechanisms, improved visual content detection systems, and training procedures that explicitly address visual jailbreaks. The paper also motivates deeper investigation into how harmful concepts can be expressed across different modalities, informing the design of truly multimodal safety frameworks rather than projecting single-modality safety onto multimodal systems.
